questions.text: Your company operates a multi-tenant SaaS application that runs on GKE clusters in four Google Cloud regions. The security team requires that network policies, pod security standards, and resource limit ranges are enforced uniformly across every cluster. Recently, an internal audit revealed that two of the four clusters had outdated network policies because an engineer forgot to apply a change to all clusters after updating the configuration. The security team wants a solution that prevents this kind of drift from happening in the future and automatically corrects any manual changes made directly to a cluster. What should you do?

options.text: Set up a Cloud Scheduler job that triggers a Cloud Run Function every hour to compare each cluster's configuration against a baseline stored in Cloud Storage and reapply any differences
options.explanation:
Config Sync is purpose-built for exactly this problem. It runs as a controller on each GKE cluster and continuously watches a Git repository that serves as the single source of truth for Kubernetes configurations. Whenever a change is committed to the repository, Config Sync detects it and applies the update to every enrolled cluster automatically. Critically, Config Sync also monitors the live state of each cluster and reverts any manual changes that deviate from what is defined in Git. This means if an engineer modifies a network policy directly on one cluster, Config Sync will detect the drift and restore the correct configuration without anyone needing to intervene. This continuous reconciliation loop is what distinguishes Config Sync from other approaches and directly addresses the audit finding.

A Cloud Build pipeline that applies configurations on each commit handles the deployment side of the problem, but it does not monitor clusters for drift after the deployment completes. If someone manually changes a network policy on a cluster between commits, Cloud Build has no way to detect or correct that change until the next pipeline run is triggered. A Cloud Run Function running on an hourly schedule could detect drift at each check interval, but it would miss any changes made between checks, and building reliable reconciliation logic from scratch is significant engineering effort for functionality that Config Sync provides natively. Using Terraform modules on a weekly schedule has even larger gaps in drift detection. A full week could pass before a misconfiguration is caught and corrected, which is far too slow for security-critical policies like network policies and pod security standards. Weekly application also means any manual fix applied mid-week would be overwritten on the next scheduled run regardless of whether it was intentional.

questions.text: Your company operates a healthcare scheduling platform backed by a Cloud SQL for MySQL instance that stores appointment records, patient intake forms, and billing transactions. A recent incident caused data corruption in several tables, and the database team was unable to restore the database to the exact moment before the corruption occurred. Management has asked you to configure the Cloud SQL instance so that in any future incident, the team can recover the database to a precise point in time with minimal data loss. Which two features should you configure?

options.text: Enable high availability with automatic failover on the Cloud SQL instance
options.explanation:
These two features are both required to enable point-in-time recovery on a Cloud SQL for MySQL instance. Automated backups create a full snapshot of the database on a regular schedule, which provides the baseline state that any recovery operation starts from. Binary logging records every individual write transaction that happens between those snapshots, creating a continuous record of changes. When you need to recover from an incident like the data corruption described in this question, you restore the most recent automated backup and then replay the binary logs up to the exact moment before the corruption occurred. This combination allows the database team to recover to a specific second rather than being limited to the last full backup, which is what makes point-in-time recovery possible.

Enabling high availability with automatic failover protects against instance-level downtime by maintaining a standby replica in a separate zone that takes over if the primary fails. However, failover preserves the current state of the database at the moment of the failure, which means if the data is already corrupted, the standby replica will contain the same corruption. It does not provide any ability to roll back to a point in time before the problem occurred. A same-region read replica continuously replicates data from the primary instance, but it replicates everything including corrupted data. If bad writes reach the primary, they propagate to the replica, so a read replica cannot help you recover to a state before the corruption happened. Creating a Cloud Run Function that exports the database to Cloud Storage every 30 minutes provides periodic snapshots, but it leaves a gap of up to 30 minutes of data that could be lost between exports. Point-in-time recovery using automated backups and binary logs can recover to within seconds of the incident rather than losing up to half an hour of transactions.

questions.text: Your company is preparing for a compliance audit that requires full visibility into the software supply chain for all containerized applications deployed to production. The audit team has specified three controls that must be in place: every container image must be scanned for known vulnerabilities before deployment, every image must have a verifiable record of how and where it was built, and every image must include a machine-readable inventory of all packages and libraries it contains. You need to implement these controls using Google Cloud services. What should you do?

options.text: Use Cloud Build to build images and push them to Artifact Registry, then configure Security Command Center to scan images and generate the build records and package inventories
options.explanation:
Artifact Analysis is the Google Cloud service that directly addresses all three audit controls. It automatically scans container images stored in Artifact Registry against public vulnerability databases to identify known CVEs. It generates and stores build provenance metadata that records where and how each image was built, giving auditors a verifiable chain of custody. It also produces a software bill of materials that lists all packages and libraries contained in the image. Cloud Build handles the image creation step, and Artifact Registry provides the secure storage layer. Together, these three services form a complete pipeline from build through analysis that satisfies each of the audit requirements without needing additional tools.

Security Command Center is a platform for aggregating and analyzing security findings across your Google Cloud environment, but it does not perform container image scanning or generate build provenance and SBOMs on its own. It can surface findings that originate from other services like Artifact Analysis, but it is a consumer of that data rather than the source. Binary Authorization is a policy enforcement tool that controls which container images are allowed to be deployed to GKE based on attestations. It checks whether an image has been approved, but it does not scan for vulnerabilities, generate provenance records, or produce package inventories. It operates at deployment time rather than at the analysis stage the audit is asking about. Cloud Asset Inventory tracks and catalogs Google Cloud resources like instances, buckets, and IAM policies across your organization. It does not inspect the contents of container images or generate any of the three artifacts the audit requires.
